chemistry-ssc-science-chemistry-board-qu.explanation: Atomic number = number of protons in nucleus.
Mass number A = Z + N (protons + neutrons).
Isotopes have same atomic number but different mass numbers.
11 electrons → 2,8,1.
Increasing nuclear charge pulls electrons closer → radius decreases.
Higher nuclear charge → more tightly held electrons → IE increases.

chemistry-ssc-science-chemistry-board-qu.correctOption: Across period to right and up the group
chemistry-ssc-science-chemistry-board-qu.explanation: Most EN at top-right (excluding noble gases); increases right & up.
Potassium (K) is Group 1 alkali metal.
Chlorine (Cl) is a halogen (Group 17).

chemistry-ssc-science-chemistry-board-qu.correctOption: Inertness due to full valence shell
chemistry-ssc-science-chemistry-board-qu.explanation: Noble gases have full valence shells → low reactivity.
F- (9+1), O2- (8+2), Na+ (11-1) all have 10 electrons → isoelectronic.
Atomic number 16 corresponds to sulfur.
Oxygen usually forms 2 bonds (valency 2).
Metals are on left; K is metal whereas Cl is nonmetal.

chemistry-ssc-science-chemistry-board-qu.correctOption: Energy change when an atom gains an electron
chemistry-ssc-science-chemistry-board-qu.explanation: Electron affinity = energy change on gaining an electron (often negative).
Fluorine is most electronegative element.
Chlorine gains one electron to form Cl- (chloride).
2+8+7=17 → atomic number 17 → Chlorine.
Across period, radius decreases → Al (to the right) smallest among listed.
Transition metal iron shows multiple oxidation states.
Mg loses two electrons → Mg2+; O2- balances charge.
Boron shows properties between metals and nonmetals → metalloid.
A large jump indicates removal of core electron after valence removed.
Alkali metal K loses one electron → K+.
Sodium ions produce intense yellow flame.
